label: Automatic Speech Recognition
definition: The task of transcribing spoken audio into text, the input side of every voice interface and a bridge from the messy analog world into the text language models work in. Modern systems are end-to-end encoder-decoder transformers like Whisper, trained on vast, diverse audio-text pairs, which is what makes them multilingual and robust to accents and noise out of the box.

body.text: Automatic speech recognition turns sound into words. It is the input side of every voice interface, and a key bridge between the messy analog world and the text that language models work in. The audio is converted to a spectral representation (a mel spectrogram), and the system maps that sequence of audio frames to a sequence of words, a hard problem because speech is continuous, variable, noisy, and full of accents and homophones.

The field moved through distinct eras. Classical systems combined hidden Markov models for the acoustics with n-gram language models for the text, in elaborate pipelines; deep learning replaced the acoustics with neural networks and connectionist temporal classification; and the current generation is end-to-end, an encoder reading the audio and a decoder generating text, exactly the transformer sequence-to-sequence setup used for translation. Whisper is the well-known example, trained weakly-supervised on a huge, diverse web corpus of audio and transcripts, which is what makes it multilingual and robust out of the box.
This is directly relevant to this project: the ingestion worker uses Whisper to transcribe videos that lack captions, turning spoken content into text the corpus and classifier can work with.

The remaining hard cases are background noise, overlapping speakers, heavy accents, rare proper nouns, code-switching between languages, and the latency demands of real-time transcription, which is why streaming ASR and speaker diarization are active areas alongside raw accuracy.

image.alt: Diagram of the Whisper speech model. On the left, examples of multitask training data: English transcription, any-to-English speech translation, non-English transcription, and no-speech audio. In the center, audio becomes a log-mel spectrogram that passes through transformer encoder blocks, and a transformer decoder with cross attention emits tokens. At the bottom, the multitask training format threads special tokens for language identification, transcription versus translation, and timestamps through one sequence.
image.caption: Modern automatic speech recognition is a sequence-to-sequence transformer: the input waveform becomes a log-mel spectrogram that the encoder reads, and the decoder generates the transcript token by token. Whisper folds transcription, translation, language identification, and timestamps into one model by prefixing the target sequence with special task tokens.
image.credit: Radford, Alec and Kim, Jong Wook and Xu, Tao and Brockman, Greg and McLeavey, Christine and Sutskever, Ilya (OpenAI)
